heroOverview: Anencephaly is a lethal neural tube defect in which the embryonic neural tube fails to close at its rostral end between the 23rd and 26th day after conception, leaving the developing brain exposed to amniotic fluid. The result is the absence of most or all of the cerebral hemispheres, the overlying calvarium, and scalp. It is one of the most severe structural malformations compatible with a completed pregnancy: the fetus is either stillborn or survives only hours to days after birth. Global birth prevalence is approximately 1 per 1,000 pregnancies, though this varies widely by geography and folate status; in some high-risk populations before fortification programmes began, rates reached 4 per 1,000. Periconceptional folic acid supplementation reduces the risk by up to 70% and is the single most effective preventive measure.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 1,000 pregnancies globally; higher in folate-deficient populations (CDC, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Affects fetuses of any sex; female fetuses are affected about 3 times more often than male fetuses
keyStats.avgAge: Defect arises at 23–26 days post-conception; detected prenatally or at birth
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 200,000–300,000 affected pregnancies worldwide each year before termination or loss

definition: Anencephaly (ICD-10: Q00.0) is a major congenital malformation of the central nervous system resulting from failure of the anterior neural tube to close during early embryogenesis, typically between day 23 and day 26 of gestation. The cerebral cortex, basal ganglia, thalamus, and overlying skull and skin are absent or rudimentary, although the brainstem and portions of the cerebellum may be partially preserved. The exposed neural tissue degenerates on contact with amniotic fluid, leaving a disorganised mass called the area cerebrovasculosa. Anencephaly belongs to the broader category of neural tube defects (NTDs), which also includes spina bifida and encephalocele, and shares the same primary risk factor — periconceptional folate deficiency. The condition is uniformly fatal; no medical or surgical intervention can restore absent brain tissue. It falls under the specialist domains of clinical genetics and maternal-fetal medicine, with neonatology and palliative care involved after delivery. The pathogenesis involves both genetic susceptibility — including variants in folate-pathway genes such as MTHFR — and environmental factors, making it a classic multifactorial malformation.

typesClassification.description: Partial absence of the brain and skull; the brainstem is present and some diencephalic structures may be identifiable. This form may be compatible with brief survival after birth.
Complete absence of the forebrain and calvarium; only rudimentary brainstem tissue remains. Stillbirth or death within hours of delivery is the usual outcome.

A severe variant in which both anterior and posterior neural tube fail to close, producing combined anencephaly with an open spine; it is classified separately as Q00.1 but forms a continuous spectrum.

primarySymptoms: Absence of the cranial vault (calvarium) above the orbits, visible on prenatal ultrasound from the 10th–14th week of gestation as a characteristic 'frog-eye' appearance when viewed in coronal section.
Absent or severely reduced cerebral hemispheres, with only a vascular haemorrhagic mass (area cerebrovasculosa) visible where cortical tissue should be.
Prominent, wide-set orbits that give the fetal face a distinctive appearance on second-trimester ultrasonography.
Polyhydramnios in approximately 50% of affected pregnancies, caused by impaired fetal swallowing due to absent cortical control.
Elevated maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein (MSAFP) in the second trimester, reflecting leakage of AFP from the exposed neural tissue.
Elevated amniotic fluid acetylcholinesterase, a more specific marker for open NTDs detected on amniocentesis.
Absent or absent-appearing skull bones above the supraorbital ridge on fetal radiograph or CT in the newborn period.
Absence of normal behavioural responses, purposeful movement, or crying in a liveborn infant beyond basic brainstem reflexes.
Apnoea or severely irregular breathing reflecting brainstem dysfunction in liveborn neonates.
Low birth weight in some cases, partly related to polyhydramnios-associated preterm delivery.
earlyWarningSigns: Elevated maternal serum AFP on routine second-trimester screening (typically between 15 and 20 weeks of gestation).
Absence of the calvarium on first-trimester nuchal translucency ultrasound at 11–14 weeks.
Failure to visualise a normal biparietal diameter measurement on routine anomaly scan.
Acrania (absence of skull bones) noted as an early precursor lesion before 14 weeks, before the brain tissue degenerates.
emergencySigns: Severe respiratory distress in a liveborn infant, reflecting brainstem instability.
Signs of sepsis in a neonate with exposed neural tissue.
Maternal complications from untreated polyhydramnios, including preterm labour, placental abruption, or uterine overdistension.
Fetal distress during labour in a known anencephalic pregnancy, requiring urgent obstetric assessment.

causes.description: Inadequate folate around the time of neural tube closure impairs one-carbon metabolism needed for DNA synthesis and methylation. Randomised trials show that supplementation with 400 mcg of folic acid daily before and during early pregnancy reduces NTD risk by 50–70%.

Homozygous or compound heterozygous variants in the MTHFR gene — particularly the 677C>T polymorphism — reduce methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase activity by 30–70%, impairing folate metabolism and raising NTD risk independently of dietary intake.

Hyperglycaemia in early pregnancy, particularly in the first 6–8 weeks, is associated with a 2–3-fold increased risk of major structural malformations including NTDs, likely through oxidative stress disrupting neuroepithelial signalling during closure.

Valproic acid inhibits folate metabolism and is among the highest-risk teratogens for NTDs; prenatal exposure raises risk approximately 10–20-fold. Other antifolates including methotrexate, carbamazepine, and phenytoin carry smaller but significant increases.

Core body temperature elevations above 38.9°C during weeks 3–4 post-conception — from fever, hot tub use, or sauna — disrupt neuroepithelial apoptosis and proliferation, and have been associated with a 2–3-fold increased NTD risk in epidemiological studies.

Trisomy 18, trisomy 13, and certain chromosomal deletions are found in a minority of NTD cases. Single-gene causes account for a small fraction and include mutations in genes regulating planar cell polarity and neural plate bending (VANGL1, CELSR1, SCRIB).

riskFactors.description: The most important modifiable risk. Women who do not take 400–800 mcg of folic acid daily before conception and through the first trimester have a significantly higher risk; women with a prior affected pregnancy are advised to take 4–5 mg daily.

Having one child with any NTD raises the recurrence risk to approximately 2–5%, compared with a background risk below 0.1% in the general population. Having two affected pregnancies raises risk further to approximately 10–15%.
Type 1 and type 2 diabetes confer a 2–3-fold increased NTD risk; tight periconceptional glycaemic control (HbA1c below 6.5%) substantially reduces but does not eliminate this risk.

riskFactors.factor: Valproate or other antifolate drug use
riskFactors.description: Valproate carries the highest teratogenic risk among anticonvulsants; women of childbearing age who need anticonvulsant therapy should discuss switching to a lower-risk agent before conception.
Homozygous carriers have reduced MTHFR enzyme activity and modestly increased NTD risk; high-dose folate supplementation compensates for much of this metabolic deficit.

riskFactors.factor: Low socioeconomic status and poor diet quality
riskFactors.description: Diets low in folate-rich foods — leafy greens, legumes, fortified grains — are more common in food-insecure populations and underlie geographic disparities in NTD prevalence.
Obesity is independently associated with a 1.5–2-fold increased NTD risk, possibly through impaired folate bioavailability, hyperglycaemia, and inflammatory mediators.

Historically high rates in Ireland, northern China, and parts of South Asia reflect dietary folate patterns; mandatory folic acid food fortification has substantially reduced rates in North America, Australia, and many other regions.

affectedDemographics: Fetuses of all ethnicities, with highest rates in populations without mandatory folate fortification
Female fetuses approximately 3 times more often than male fetuses (reason unclear but consistent across studies)
Pregnancies in women with pre-existing diabetes, regardless of type
Fetuses of women on valproate or other antifolate medications during early pregnancy
Families with a prior NTD-affected pregnancy, who face 2–5% recurrence risk
Fetuses in low-income settings with limited access to prenatal vitamins or fortified foods
diagnosisOverview: Anencephaly is almost always diagnosed prenatally. Routine first-trimester ultrasonography between 11 and 14 weeks identifies acrania — absent skull bones above the orbits — as the earliest sign; by 14 weeks the overlying neural tissue degenerates and the typical anencephalic appearance becomes unmistakable. Second-trimester anomaly scans at 18–22 weeks confirm the diagnosis with near-100% sensitivity in skilled hands. Maternal serum AFP screening, offered between 15 and 20 weeks, is typically markedly elevated (greater than 2.5 multiples of the median); amniotic fluid AFP and acetylcholinesterase are elevated when amniocentesis is performed and can distinguish NTDs from other causes of raised MSAFP. Fetal karyotype or chromosomal microarray is offered to identify co-existing chromosomal anomalies such as trisomy 18 or 13, which are found in approximately 15–20% of NTD cases. A detailed fetal anomaly scan assesses for additional malformations. When the diagnosis is made late or at delivery, postnatal clinical examination and skull radiographs are diagnostic. Genetic counselling is offered to all families to discuss recurrence risk, preventive strategies, and options in future pregnancies.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies acrania — absence of the bony calvarium — as the earliest ultrasonographic precursor of anencephaly, before cortical tissue has fully degenerated.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A transabdominal or transvaginal ultrasound scan performed with the fetus in a suitable position; the sonographer measures the head and assesses skull contour and brain tissue. Takes approximately 20–30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the absence of cerebral hemispheres and calvarium, assesses for associated malformations, and evaluates amniotic fluid volume.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard obstetric ultrasound; the finding of absent cranial vault and exposed brain tissue is visually unmistakable to a trained sonographer.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects elevated AFP from the exposed neural tissue; values typically exceed 5–10 multiples of the median in anencephaly.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from the maternal arm between 15 and 20 weeks; results are available within a few days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides a specific marker (acetylcholinesterase) that distinguishes open NTDs from other causes of elevated MSAFP, and allows chromosomal analysis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fine needle is inserted through the maternal abdomen under ultrasound guidance to draw a small sample of amniotic fluid; mild cramping may occur; chromosomal results take 10–14 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects underlying chromosomal abnormalities (trisomy 18, trisomy 13, deletion syndromes) present in approximately 15–20% of cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on amniotic fluid or chorionic villus sample; results take 7–14 days for microarray or 2–3 weeks for standard karyotype.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides additional anatomical detail about residual brainstem and posterior fossa structures when ultrasonographic views are limited by fetal position or maternal body habitus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The mother lies in an MRI scanner, usually in the second or third trimester; no radiation is used; the scan takes about 30–45 minutes.

treatmentOverview: There is no treatment that can restore absent brain tissue; the goal of management after diagnosis is to support the family through the pregnancy and the neonatal period with accurate information, compassionate counselling, and planned perinatal care. Parents are offered the full range of options: continuation of pregnancy with palliative neonatal care, induction of labour at a gestational age chosen by the family, or, where legally available, termination of pregnancy. Many families choose to continue the pregnancy and participate in perinatal palliative care programmes that focus on comfort, dignity, and the opportunity to spend time with the infant. A small number of families donate organs from anencephalic neonates, which is ethically and legally permitted in many jurisdictions under specific protocols. Obstetric management addresses the risk of polyhydramnios, malpresentation, and prolonged pregnancy; amnioreduction may be offered for severe polyhydramnios causing maternal discomfort or preterm labour risk. Following delivery, neonatal care is comfort-focused: warmth, gentle handling, skin-to-skin contact, and symptom management. Ventilation and cardiopulmonary resuscitation are not appropriate given the condition's lethality and the absence of any capacity for conscious experience. Genetic counselling before the next pregnancy is essential: women are advised to begin high-dose folic acid (4–5 mg daily) at least 3 months before attempting conception.

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose folic acid (for future pregnancy prevention)
medicalTreatments.description: Women with a prior affected pregnancy are prescribed 4–5 mg of folic acid daily, beginning at least one month and ideally three months before conception, and continuing through the first trimester. This is distinct from standard supplementation in the general population.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces recurrence risk of NTDs by approximately 70% compared with no supplementation, based on the landmark MRC Vitamin Study (1991).

medicalTreatments.name: Amnioreduction for polyhydramnios
medicalTreatments.description: When excess amniotic fluid causes maternal discomfort, dyspnoea, or significant risk of preterm labour, serial removal of amniotic fluid under ultrasound guidance can relieve symptoms and reduce uterine overdistension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective symptomatic relief in approximately 80–90% of procedures; does not alter fetal prognosis but improves maternal comfort.

medicalTreatments.name: Perinatal palliative care programme
medicalTreatments.description: A structured interdisciplinary plan agreed before delivery, specifying the setting, personnel, and comfort measures for the neonate. It includes pain management, temperature regulation, and family support, and is delivered by neonatology and palliative care teams.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Associated with improved parental satisfaction and bereavement outcomes in approximately 90% of families in published palliative care programme audits.

medicalTreatments.name: Induction of labour (timing by parental choice)
medicalTreatments.description: Induction is offered when parents wish to end the pregnancy after diagnosis and the gestational age allows. Method depends on gestational age and cervical status; medical or surgical methods are used according to standard obstetric protocols.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful delivery achieved in essentially 100% of cases with appropriate obstetric management; fetal outcome is not altered.

alternativeTreatments.name: Folate-rich dietary optimisation
alternativeTreatments.description: Increasing intake of dark leafy vegetables, legumes, and fortified cereals in the periconceptional period complements supplement use, though diet alone cannot reliably supply the 400–800 mcg dose shown to be protective in clinical trials.

alternativeTreatments.name: Organ donation
alternativeTreatments.description: With strict ethics oversight and parental consent, organs from anencephalic neonates — primarily kidneys — may be transplanted under protocols established in some jurisdictions. This is not a treatment but a decision option families may wish to discuss.

whySeeSpecialist: A maternal-fetal medicine (MFM) specialist should be involved as soon as anencephaly is suspected or confirmed to co-ordinate detailed ultrasonographic assessment, discuss all management options, and plan delivery. A clinical geneticist or genetic counsellor provides recurrence risk estimates, MTHFR testing, and pre-pregnancy planning. Neonatology and paediatric palliative care teams are involved when the family chooses to continue the pregnancy, to ensure a dignified and supported birth experience.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek care at a centre with a dedicated fetal medicine unit staffed by maternal-fetal medicine specialists who have experience managing lethal fetal anomalies. The centre should offer on-site genetic counselling, amniocentesis, chromosomal microarray, and an integrated perinatal palliative care programme. Ask specifically about experience with birth planning for anencephaly and whether the team can support families choosing continuation of pregnancy.
preventionStrategies: Take 400 mcg of folic acid daily for at least one month before conception and during the first 12 weeks of pregnancy; women with a prior NTD-affected pregnancy should take 4–5 mg daily.
Consume folate-rich foods daily — dark leafy vegetables, legumes, fortified cereals and breads.
Achieve tight glycaemic control before conception if you have diabetes; aim for HbA1c below 6.5%.
Discuss with a specialist any use of valproate, methotrexate, or carbamazepine before attempting pregnancy, as lower-risk alternatives are often available.
Avoid hyperthermia in early pregnancy: treat fever promptly with paracetamol, and avoid hot tubs and saunas in weeks 3–8.
Support mandatory folic acid fortification of staple foods, the single most cost-effective population-level NTD prevention strategy.
lifestyleModifications: Begin a prenatal vitamin containing 400–800 mcg folic acid before stopping contraception.
Maintain a balanced diet rich in dark green vegetables, lentils, beans, and fortified cereals in the months before and after conception.
Manage blood glucose carefully if you have diabetes, using a continuous glucose monitor or regular home testing.
Avoid alcohol and smoking, which impair folate absorption and raise general teratogenic risk.
Seek immediate medical review if fever develops during the first eight weeks of pregnancy.

recommended: Spinach, kale, broccoli, and other dark leafy greens (rich in natural folate)
Lentils, black beans, chickpeas (among the highest folate-density foods per serving)
Fortified breakfast cereals providing 100% of the daily folate requirement per serving
Fortified bread and pasta where national fortification programmes are in place
Oranges and orange juice as an additional folate source
avoid: Excessive alcohol, which inhibits folate absorption and raises plasma homocysteine
Liver and liver products in high quantities (contains retinol at potentially teratogenic levels when combined with other risk factors)
Unpasteurised foods (food safety risk in early pregnancy, not specifically anti-folate)

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate exercise before and during pregnancy is encouraged and does not raise NTD risk. Avoid saunas, hot yoga, and hot tubs during the first trimester, particularly weeks 3–8, because of hyperthermia risk. Pregnant women diagnosed with anencephaly should follow general obstetric guidance regarding activity, with modifications for polyhydramnios if present.
dailyManagement: Families who continue the pregnancy should engage with the palliative care team early to design a birth plan.
Monitor for signs of polyhydramnios (rapid abdominal growth, breathlessness) and report them promptly.
Attend all scheduled fetal medicine and obstetric appointments for growth monitoring and wellbeing assessment.
Access bereavement and psychological support services; perinatal loss is associated with significant grief and post-traumatic stress.
Plan the next pregnancy with a clinical geneticist and begin high-dose folic acid supplementation well before conception.
prognosis: Anencephaly is uniformly lethal. Approximately 75% of affected fetuses are stillborn; of those born alive, the great majority die within the first hours of life, and survival beyond 24 hours is uncommon. A small number of infants with the meroanencephaly variant have survived for days or, rarely, weeks, but no case of long-term meaningful survival has been documented. The prognosis for families who receive genetic counselling and begin high-dose folic acid supplementation before their next pregnancy is substantially better: recurrence risk falls to below 1% with adherence to the 4–5 mg prophylactic regimen. The psychological impact on families is profound, and bereavement outcomes are improved by access to specialist perinatal palliative care and psychological support services.
recoveryTimeline: There is no recovery timeline for the infant. For the family, acute grief typically peaks in the first weeks after loss. Most bereaved parents describe the first 3–6 months as the most intense period, with gradual adaptation over 1–2 years; a minority experience prolonged grief disorder or post-traumatic stress requiring professional support. Planning a subsequent pregnancy is typically recommended after one normal menstrual cycle has passed physiologically, but emotionally many families benefit from waiting 6–12 months.
complications: Polyhydramnios — accumulation of excess amniotic fluid in approximately 50% of cases, increasing maternal risk of preterm labour, dyspnoea, and uterine overdistension.
Malpresentation and obstructed labour — the abnormal cranial shape increases the risk of brow, face, or shoulder presentation.

Prolonged pregnancy — the fetal adrenal gland contributes to the hormonal cascade triggering labour; absence of functional hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal tissue in anencephaly can delay the onset of labour significantly.
Maternal psychological morbidity — clinically significant depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorder occur in 20–30% of mothers following perinatal loss from a lethal anomaly.
Recurrence in future pregnancies — without high-dose periconceptional folic acid supplementation, recurrence risk is 2–5%; with it, risk falls to below 1%.

supportResources.name: CDC — Folic Acid and Neural Tube Defects
supportResources.description: Evidence-based guidance on folic acid supplementation for NTD prevention from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: NHS — Anencephaly
supportResources.description: Plain-English overview of anencephaly, diagnosis, management options, and bereavement support from NHS England.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Neural Tube Defects
supportResources.description: NICHD overview of neural tube defects including anencephaly, covering causes, prevention, and research.

sources.title: MRC Vitamin Study Research Group. Prevention of neural tube defects: results of the Medical Research Council Vitamin Study. Lancet, 1991
Copp AJ et al. The genetic basis of mammalian neurulation. Nature Reviews Genetics, 2003

confusedWithConditions.name: Encephalocele
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Encephalocele is herniation of brain tissue through a skull defect, with the calvarium largely intact; anencephaly involves complete absence of the calvarium and cerebral hemispheres.

confusedWithConditions.name: Craniorachischisis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Craniorachischisis extends the defect down the spine as well as the head; anencephaly alone involves only the anterior neural tube failure without an open spinal lesion.

confusedWithConditions.name: Holoprosencephaly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Holoprosencephaly involves failure of the forebrain to divide into hemispheres, but the skull is intact and varying degrees of brain tissue are present, unlike the absent calvarium of anencephaly.

confusedWithConditions.name: Septo-optic dysplasia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Septo-optic dysplasia causes absence of the septum pellucidum and optic nerve hypoplasia with a largely intact skull, and is compatible with survival; anencephaly is invariably lethal.

relatedConditions.name: Craniorachischisis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most severe neural tube defect, combining anencephaly with an open spine; shares all major risk factors and the same embryological failure of neural tube closure.

relatedConditions.name: Iniencephaly
relatedConditions.relevance: A related lethal NTD involving retroflexion of the spine and open skull at the occiput; similar epidemiology and prevention strategies.

relatedConditions.name: Encephalocele
relatedConditions.relevance: A milder form of neural tube closure failure in which brain tissue herniates through a skull defect; same risk factors as anencephaly including folate deficiency.

relatedConditions.name: Spina bifida
relatedConditions.relevance: The most prevalent neural tube defect, caused by posterior neural tube failure; shares identical prevention strategies and risk factors with anencephaly.

relatedConditions.name: Microcephaly
relatedConditions.relevance: Another major congenital brain malformation diagnosed prenatally; both conditions require specialist fetal medicine assessment and genetic counselling.

faqs.question: What is anencephaly?
faqs.answer: Anencephaly is a birth defect in which the brain and skull do not form properly during early pregnancy, leaving the cerebral hemispheres and much of the skull absent. It occurs when the embryonic neural tube fails to close at the head end around day 23–26 after conception. It is invariably fatal.

faqs.question: Is anencephaly always fatal?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anencephaly is uniformly lethal. About 75% of affected fetuses are stillborn. Of those born alive, almost all die within hours of birth. No medical or surgical treatment can restore absent brain tissue.

faqs.question: What causes anencephaly?
faqs.answer: The main cause is failure of the anterior neural tube to close during the first four weeks of pregnancy. Key risk factors include low folic acid intake before and in early pregnancy, maternal diabetes, valproate use, fever in early pregnancy, and certain genetic variants such as MTHFR 677C>T. Most cases are multifactorial.

faqs.question: Can folic acid prevent anencephaly?
faqs.answer: Yes, to a significant degree. Taking 400 mcg of folic acid daily beginning at least one month before conception and continuing through the first trimester reduces neural tube defect risk by up to 70%. Women with a prior affected pregnancy should take the higher dose of 4–5 mg daily.

faqs.question: How is anencephaly diagnosed during pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Most cases are identified on routine first-trimester ultrasound at 11–14 weeks, when the absent skull (acrania) is visible. The diagnosis is confirmed on the second-trimester anomaly scan at 18–22 weeks. Elevated maternal serum AFP and amniocentesis are used as additional confirmatory tests.

faqs.question: Can anencephaly be detected in the first trimester?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acrania — absence of the bony skull above the eye sockets — is visible from approximately 11 weeks on a skilled first-trimester ultrasound. By 13–14 weeks the absent brain tissue makes the diagnosis unmistakable. This is earlier than most other major structural anomalies.

faqs.question: What are the options when anencephaly is diagnosed prenatally?
faqs.answer: Families may choose to continue the pregnancy with perinatal palliative care, or, where legally available, to terminate the pregnancy. There is no corrective treatment. Parents who continue the pregnancy are supported by a specialist team to plan a dignified birth, including the option of holding and spending time with the baby.

faqs.question: What is the recurrence risk for anencephaly?
faqs.answer: After one affected pregnancy, the recurrence risk for any neural tube defect is approximately 2–5%. Taking 4–5 mg of folic acid daily from at least one month before the next conception reduces this risk by roughly 70%. Genetic counselling before the next pregnancy is strongly recommended.

faqs.question: Can anencephaly be caused by genetics?
faqs.answer: Anencephaly has a strong genetic component. Variants in folate-pathway genes such as MTHFR 677C>T reduce the body's ability to use folate. Chromosomal abnormalities — especially trisomy 18 and trisomy 13 — are found in about 15–20% of cases. Most cases, however, are multifactorial, involving both genetic and environmental factors.

faqs.question: Does anencephaly cause pain to the baby?
faqs.answer: Because the cerebral cortex — the part of the brain required to consciously perceive pain — is absent, anencephalic infants are not believed to experience pain in the way a normal infant does. Brainstem reflexes may cause them to startle or withdraw, but these are reflexive responses rather than conscious suffering.

faqs.question: Can anencephalic babies donate organs?
faqs.answer: In some countries, with strict ethical oversight and full parental consent, organs from anencephalic neonates — primarily kidneys — can be donated for transplantation. The decision is entirely voluntary, and families are never pressured. Policies vary significantly by country and institution.

faqs.question: Is anencephaly related to spina bifida?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both are neural tube defects caused by failure of different parts of the embryonic neural tube to close. Anencephaly involves the head end; spina bifida involves the spine. They share the same primary risk factor — low periconceptional folic acid — and the same prevention strategies.

faqs.question: How common is anencephaly?
faqs.answer: Globally, anencephaly affects approximately 1 in 1,000 pregnancies, though rates vary widely. In countries with mandatory folic acid food fortification, rates have fallen by 25–50%. Female fetuses are affected about 3 times more often than male fetuses.

faqs.question: What happens to the pregnancy when anencephaly is diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Many families choose to continue the pregnancy and participate in a perinatal palliative care programme. Others choose induction of labour or termination where legally available. Continuing the pregnancy does not worsen maternal health in most cases, though polyhydramnios — too much amniotic fluid — occurs in about half of affected pregnancies and can cause maternal discomfort.

faqs.question: Is anencephaly painful for the mother during pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Physically, anencephaly does not directly cause the mother pain. However, the polyhydramnios that occurs in roughly 50% of cases can cause abdominal distension, breathlessness, and discomfort. Prolonged pregnancy is also more common because the fetus lacks the hormonal signalling that normally triggers labour onset.

faqs.question: What is the difference between anencephaly and hydranencephaly?
faqs.answer: Anencephaly means the cerebral hemispheres and skull never form. Hydranencephaly is a different condition in which the hemispheres initially form but are then largely destroyed by vascular or infectious injury, leaving fluid-filled sacs within an intact skull. Both are severe, but they have different causes and prenatal appearances.

faqs.question: Why is anencephaly more common in female fetuses?
faqs.answer: Female fetuses are about 3 times more likely to be affected than male fetuses, but the reason for this sex difference is not well understood. Proposed explanations include differences in folate metabolism, hormonal influences on neural tube closure, or selective survival of affected male fetuses to term, but none has been definitively proven.

faqs.question: What psychological support is available after an anencephaly diagnosis?
faqs.answer: Specialist perinatal bereavement counselling, psychological therapy, and peer support groups are available through most fetal medicine units and children's hospices. Access to support before and after birth is associated with better bereavement outcomes. Many hospitals have dedicated bereavement midwives or social workers.

faqs.question: Does taking folic acid in early pregnancy help if already pregnant?
faqs.answer: The neural tube closes by day 26 of embryonic development, which is before most women know they are pregnant. For folic acid to prevent anencephaly, it must be taken before conception and in the very first weeks of pregnancy. Starting folic acid after the tube has already closed does not prevent NTDs in the current pregnancy but is still beneficial for general fetal development.

faqs.question: Can anencephaly be caused by a medication?
faqs.answer: Yes. Valproate, used to treat epilepsy and bipolar disorder, raises neural tube defect risk approximately 10–20-fold. Methotrexate, carbamazepine, and phenytoin also carry elevated risk. Women of childbearing age taking these medications should discuss switching to safer alternatives before planning a pregnancy.

regionalNames.name: Dimag nahi hona (brain absent birth defect)

metaDescription: Anencephaly is a lethal neural tube defect where the brain and skull fail to form. Learn about prenatal diagnosis, folic acid prevention, and care options.
